comments:
Summary:
This paper studies patrolling/security games with spatially uncertain alarm signals and multiple mobile defensive resources. The authors (i) define the minimum covering placement problem (minimum number of resources needed so every target can be reached within its deadline), proving log-APX-completeness on general graphs and polynomial-time solvability on trees and cycles (with an explicit bottom-up algorithm); (ii) introduce three coordination schemes for signal response (full, partial, none), propose solution oracles (including a row-generation approach for the full-coordination case and iterative/heuristic schemes for partial/no coordination), and (iii) combine these pieces into an anytime resolution approach with experimental evaluation on randomly generated urban-like instances.

Strengths:
- The problem formulation is a natural and useful generalization of single-resource alarm-aware patrolling games to the multi-resource setting, addressing a practically relevant issue.
- Theoretical contributions (complexity classification and polynomial algorithm for special graphs) are meaningful and grounded in reductions to well-known combinatorial problems (DOMINATING-SET / SET-COVER) and dynamic programming on trees.
- The paper distinguishes different coordination models (FC/PC/NC) and tailors algorithms to each, which is useful from the modeling and practical viewpoint.
- The experimental evaluation is fairly thorough within the stated limits: it studies utility and runtime trade-offs across the three oracles, investigates scalability up to 120 targets, and explores how coordination and number of resources affect performance.

Weaknesses and suggestions for improvement:
1) Strong modeling assumptions. The paper assumes no false positives and no missed detections (i.e., alarms are raised iff an attack occurs, and the alarm set support is correct). This is a substantial simplification: many real alarm systems have false positives and false negatives. Although the authors state this is future work, the current assumption should be more prominently discussed (practical implications, how results would degrade when relaxing it). Experiments should clarify how realistic the single-signal covering-all-targets setup is or include scenarios with multiple and partial-support signals.

2) Limited experimental baselines and sensitivity analysis. The experiments compare the three proposed SROs against each other but lack comparisons against simpler baselines (e.g., naive heuristics, random placement, single-resource strategies) or previous single-resource methods to better quantify gains. A sensitivity analysis w.r.t. parameters (penetration times, graph density, number of signals, varying overlap) would strengthen claims about practical performance.

3) Heuristics and theoretical guarantees. Several algorithmic choices are heuristic or iterative (LP-relaxation sampling for row generation, iterative best-response for PC-SRO). The paper reports good empirical performance but provides limited theoretical analysis of convergence properties or approximation guarantees. Where possible, include statements about worst-case behavior or at least discuss conditions under which the heuristics are expected to work well.

4) Scalability of route computation. The authors note that computing covering routes dominates runtime and limits instance sizes. More discussion of this bottleneck, possible speedups, or trade-offs (e.g., limiting route length, sampling routes) would be helpful. If available, include runtimes for the route-generation subroutine separately and more detail on how route generation scales with instance parameters.

5) Presentation issues and clarity. The paper is generally clear, but some sections are dense and would benefit from clearer algorithmic pseudocode, explicit complexity bounds for the tree algorithm, and more careful proofreading (there are duplicated/garbled lines near the end of the provided text and some minor notation clutter). Figures are referenced but not visible in the provided text — ensure all figures are clear and captions explain experimental settings.

Minor points:
- The NP-hardness and log-APX-hardness proofs are sketched succinctly and are plausible; including formal proofs in an appendix would be appreciated.
- The relationship to team-games/minmax literature is sound; however, more discussion on alternative solution techniques (e.g., correlated-team solutions or decomposition methods) could be included.

Overall assessment:
This is a solid, well-motivated contribution that advances the study of alarm-aware security games to the practically important multi-resource setting. The combination of complexity results, explicit polynomial algorithms for special topologies, and practical anytime methods is valuable. The experimental evaluation supports the claims, albeit with scope for broader baselines and parameter sweeps. I recommend acceptance and suggest the authors address the points above (especially clearer discussion of modeling assumptions, more baseline comparisons/sensitivity analysis, and additional detail on heuristic behavior and scalability).
